mnemonics.title: WCAG POUR Principles
mnemonics.content: POUR = Perceivable (can see/hear), Operable (can use), Understandable (clear), Robust (works everywhere)

mnemonics.title: Nielsen's 10 Heuristics
mnemonics.content: VISUF-EC-HEN: Visibility, Item (match real world), System status, User control, Flexibility, Error prevention, Consistency, Help, Error recovery, Navigation/Documentation

mnemonics.title: Design System Components
mnemonics.content: DSU = Design tokens + Style Guide + Components + Patterns + Documentation

acronyms.usage: International standards for web accessibility compliance
Apple's design guidelines for their platforms
Enhanced WCAG compliance requiring 7:1 contrast ratio
Comprehensive collection of reusable components and standards

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a spreadsheet - rows and columns dividing the screen into consistent sections. Use 8px or 12-column grids for alignment.
Think of a seesaw - text on one side, background on other. The farther apart in brightness, the more stable (readable) it is.
Imagine a newspaper - headline (largest, boldest), subhead (medium), body (smallest). Size and weight create importance order.
Think of a dartboard - the bullseye (44px+) is easy to hit, small corners are hard to tap. Make interactive elements large enough.
Think of breathing - spaces between elements let the design 'breathe' and reduce cognitive overload.

analogies.analogy: Like a recipe book - provides ingredients (components) and steps (patterns) to create consistent, quality 'dishes' (products).
Like a LEGO set - standardized pieces that can be combined in countless ways to build different structures.
Like wheelchair ramps in buildings - designing for accessibility initially benefits everyone, not just those with disabilities.
Like traffic rules - consistent expectations make interactions smooth and predictable for all users.
